video_title: Wall Street’s Biggest Week Starts With “The Call @ Hedgeye”
gist: Hedgeye's daily 'Call @ Hedgeye' morning meeting runs through analyst-by-analyst actionable long/short stock picks generated by combining Hedgeye's quantitative 'signal strength' list with fundamental research ('quantamental'). Headline items include PayPal's reported $53B take-private bid from Stripe and Advent (which Hedgeye's signal flagged two days early), a Nike-short/Adidas-long apparel pair trade, accelerating Las Vegas RevPAR alongside live MGM/Caesars takeout speculation, Eli Lilly's Medicare-subsidized GLP-1 opportunity, and a Conagra short predicated on a dividend cut exposing a 'fake' yield. The macro backdrop is Hedgeye's call for continued disinflation with rising odds of an August inflation re-acceleration ('Quad 3'), layered with AI-related threads on Google's Gemini 3.5 Pro launch, a cybersecurity-software rally, PJM power shortfalls, and growing enterprise adoption of cheap Chinese AI models.

insights.claim: Hedgeye has flagged CPI surprising to the downside for months, and Treasury yields made lower highs versus their May/June cycle peaks (10-year around 4.67%, 2-year peaking in June), signaling the market is positioning for broad disinflation.
insights.speaker: Keith (host)
insights.so_what: Apply a disinflation overlay to every stock and sector pick, since fighting the macro trend undermines security selection.
insights.quote: we've been talking about CPI surprising on the downside for months now

insights.claim: Hedgeye's proprietary inflation model publishes rate-of-change estimates about six weeks ahead of the official CPI release and correctly called May 2026 as inflation's cycle 'high water mark' at 4.25% before the print confirmed it.
insights.speaker: Josh Steiner (financials analyst)
insights.so_what: Front-run consensus inflation surprises by tracking rate-of-change rather than waiting for the official print.
insights.quote: our numbers uh have a very good track record of being uh rate of change

insights.claim: Hedgeye now assigns a 60% conditional probability that inflation accelerates in August while growth decelerates (a 'Quad 3' regime), up three percentage points from its prior estimate, after months of pure disinflation.
insights.speaker: Josh Steiner (financials analyst)
insights.so_what: Watch for a regime shift into Quad 3 in August, which would argue for de-risking pure disinflation trades and rotating toward inflation-resilient positioning.
insights.quote: we're now sitting at a uh 60% probability uh conditional probability that August uh will see inflation accelerate

insights.claim: Hedgeye closed its long South Korean semiconductor/DRAM position into an overnight Kospi rally of more than 6% and moved the sector to neutral, rotating the freed-up capital into cybersecurity software names.
insights.speaker: Keith (host)
insights.so_what: Treat 'neutral' as an active decision to take profits after a big move rather than chase further upside; the rotation destination (cybersecurity) is itself a signal-confirmed trade.
insights.quote: We were riding DRAM, the ticker DRAM, think semis, uh, fully loaded with South Korean semis on the long side. We got out and flipped into bug into cyber security.

insights.claim: Hedgeye combines a quantitative price/volume 'signal' with fundamental analyst views (what they call 'quantamental') to time entries and exits, arguing that a good stock idea can go unrealized for weeks if bought at the wrong time.
insights.speaker: Keith (host)
insights.so_what: Use a systematic timing signal alongside fundamental conviction rather than relying on fundamentals alone to decide when to buy or sell.
insights.quote: our job is to time markets. Our job is to use AI and fractal signals to do so.

insights.claim: Yum Brands was removed from Hedgeye's long 'signal strength' list after a brief stint, driven by a food-safety issue (contaminated lettuce, pico de gallo, and guacamole) that originated in Michigan and spread across several states.
insights.speaker: Bennett (restaurant analyst)
insights.so_what: A supply-chain food-safety scare can force an abrupt exit from an otherwise-strong same-store-sales story; watch for confirmation the issue is contained before re-entering.
insights.quote: Bad lettuce, pico, guac

insights.claim: Multinational quick-service restaurant chains such as McDonald's and Domino's face steepening same-store-sales comparisons as US consumers shift spending toward fast-casual, casual dining, and convenience-store food, a structural headwind independent of the broader macro cycle.
insights.speaker: Bennett (restaurant analyst)
insights.so_what: Avoid domestic-heavy legacy QSR names into tougher comps; the channel shift argues for owning fast-casual/convenience exposure instead.

insights.claim: Dutch Bros (the coffee chain, referenced on-air as 'Bros') is a buy at the low end of its trading range, supported by average unit volumes running above underwritten targets and management's plan to roughly double its store count — and EBITDA — by 2029.
insights.speaker: Bennett (restaurant analyst)
insights.so_what: A unit-growth story trading near the low end of its range with volumes beating underwriting targets offers asymmetric upside if the doubling plan is executed.
insights.quote: They're gonna double units by 2029 and effectively double EIDA on the way up there.

insights.claim: Conagra (referenced on-air by ticker as 'Kagra'/CA) reported flat organic sales — a 240-basis-point sequential deceleration — with volumes down 1.6% versus a 0.5% increase the prior quarter, gross margins down 130 basis points, roughly $2 billion of impairment charges, and a dividend cut in half that took the yield down from about 10%.
insights.speaker: BC (staples analyst)
insights.so_what: The dividend cut confirms the short thesis that the reported yield was masking deteriorating earnings power; stay short into further guided declines.

insights.claim: A double-digit dividend yield paired with deteriorating earnings estimates is typically a value trap rather than a value opportunity — investors should recompute the 'real' yield after cutting earnings estimates rather than trusting the headline yield, especially when short interest is also elevated.
insights.speaker: Keith (host)
insights.so_what: Screen for high dividend yield plus high short interest as a red flag before buying, not a buy signal.
insights.quote: There's usually something wrong if it has high short interest or high dividend yield

insights.claim: Las Vegas RevPAR accelerated sharply in recent weeks — up 33% for the week ended June 20, up 24% the following week, only up 3% around the July 4th holiday, and up 27% year-over-year in the most recent week — with the recovery entering its easiest comparison period and a strong event calendar (the McGregor fight, a hockey tournament) running through late July and August.
insights.speaker: gaming/leisure analyst (Jenkins)
insights.so_what: Accelerating RevPAR into easier comps supports owning Vegas-exposed gaming names heading into earnings, independent of any M&A catalyst.
insights.quote: RevPAR for the weekended 620 up 33% the week after that up 24.

insights.claim: Caesars and MGM are both live M&A/takeout candidates, with Caesars viewed as closer to a deal than MGM, layered on top of an improving Las Vegas fundamental backdrop; MGM is flagged as the more actionable buy near the low end of its trading range.
insights.speaker: gaming/leisure analyst (Jenkins)
insights.so_what: Own MGM for both the accelerating RevPAR fundamentals and optionality on a Caesars-style takeout premium.

insights.claim: Nike (short position) is only in the early stages of what is expected to be a 3-to-5-year turnaround, with excess inventory clogging the retail channel; two small athletic retailers have already gone bankrupt, and further channel destocking could cause more retailer bankruptcies and pain for competitors like On Holdings and Deckers.
insights.speaker: McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)
insights.so_what: Stay short Nike and be cautious on retailers exposed to the athletic channel glut (Dick's/Foot Locker) until inventory normalizes, likely years away.
insights.quote: I literally think it's going to be like a 3 to 5year process.

insights.claim: Adidas — the only long name in Hedgeye's athletic-apparel coverage — is gaining market share via better incremental margins and by shifting product out of the messy wholesale channel into its own direct-to-consumer channel, and is out-executing Nike specifically around World Cup product despite Nike being the official USA team sponsor.
insights.speaker: McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)
insights.so_what: Pair long Adidas against short Nike as a relative-value trade on diverging revenue ('pod one') and cash-flow ('pod two') trends.
insights.quote: Adidas is definitely out executing Nike and and and not by a little.

insights.claim: Nike holds roughly 98% market share of basketball shoes priced above $180 but has no dominant presence in women's basketball specifically, a gap Adidas is exploiting by signing viral, highly marketable WNBA player Sophie Cunningham as an endorser.
insights.speaker: McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)
insights.so_what: Women's basketball footwear is a white-space category where Adidas can build share without directly displacing Nike's core dominant position.
insights.quote: Nike's got 98% market share for basketball shoes over $180.

insights.claim: Contour Brands (ticker KTB), after divesting its Lee jeans brand, is a buy on signal strength driven by strong demand for its Wrangler denim brand and the under-monetized Helly Hansen outdoor brand (only about $125 million in sales), which it acquired roughly six months ago from a Canadian retailer that had mismanaged it.
insights.speaker: McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)
insights.so_what: A steady 'base hit' long combining one growth brand (Helly Hansen) and one annuity brand in a favorable denim cycle (Wrangler), though not expected to be an explosive mover.

insights.claim: 10x Genomics flipped from a short — covered after riding it down from roughly $70 to $7 — to a new long around $11, based on a proprietary dataset showing the genomics tools/consumables cycle is inflecting upward in a pattern comparable to prior secular waves at Affymetrix, Applied Biosystems, and Illumina.
insights.speaker: Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)
insights.so_what: An AI-enabled scientific-discovery cycle should drive sustained consumable/razor-blade demand across genomics tools names (10x Genomics, Illumina, Bruker), not just a short-term bounce.

insights.claim: Eli Lilly is flagged as the day's top actionable buy because Medicare's new 'Bridge' program will make GLP-1 weight-loss drugs accessible to Medicare beneficiaries for about $50 a month — the first time Medicare has paid for weight-loss drugs — which should durably favor a small number of consumer-facing incumbents like Lilly and Novo Nordisk over follow-on competitors given how far pricing has already fallen.
insights.speaker: Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)
insights.so_what: Buy Lilly on weakness as mass-market, Medicare-subsidized GLP-1 access expands the addressable market while limiting room for new entrants to undercut on price.
insights.quote: Medicare is rolling out a program called Bridge. It makes $50 a month glip one accessible to Medicare beneficiaries.

insights.claim: Eli Lilly's newest weight-loss compound (described by the analysts, who struggled to name it precisely on air, as a triple-action drug) is said to reduce triglycerides and help treat sleep apnea, adding a second product leg beyond its original GLP-1 franchise.
insights.speaker: Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)
insights.so_what: A broadening drug pipeline beyond a single GLP-1 compound supports the durability case for staying long Lilly.

insights.claim: U.S. Physical Therapy (USPH) is flagged as a short candidate sitting in the 'splash zone' of the same managed-care cost pressures hitting HCA and Tenet, described as a high-multiple stock with both secular and cyclical/idiosyncratic downside risk.
insights.speaker: Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)
insights.so_what: Extend the healthcare-services short thesis beyond the obvious hospital names to smaller high-multiple names exposed to the same reimbursement pressure.
insights.quote: it's a high multiple silly little, you know, stock from my view.

insights.claim: Elevance Health (ELV) reported its benefit margin rising 80 basis points to roughly 90% — described as a 'no-go zone' for managed care — while its core health-benefits operating margin (excluding the Carelon unit) fell 170 basis points; despite raising 2026 EPS guidance to at least $27, the implied second-half EPS of about $7 versus roughly $20 already booked signals a sharp deceleration.
insights.speaker: healthcare/managed-care analyst
insights.so_what: The headline guidance raise masks deteriorating core managed-care economics — rising member risk profiles and states withholding Medicaid rate increases — that will keep flowing through to hospital operators like HCA and Tenet.
insights.quote: staring at uh, 90% which is, you know, no-go zone in managed care.

insights.claim: Managed-care cost pressure and states declining to raise Medicaid reimbursement reflect a broader post-pandemic 'disinflation/deflation' unwind after years of money-printing-driven healthcare cost inflation, and this unwind will keep pressuring hospital and services companies like HCA and Tenet.
insights.speaker: healthcare/managed-care analyst
insights.so_what: Treat healthcare services as a multi-quarter drag from the same disinflation dynamic driving the broader macro call, not an isolated sector issue.

insights.claim: PayPal is reportedly receiving a buyout offer from a group including Stripe and private-equity firm Advent for $53 billion, roughly a mid-20s percent premium to its prior-day $42 billion market capitalization.
insights.speaker: Josh Steiner (financials analyst)
insights.so_what: A confirmed $53B take-private bid at a roughly 25% premium sets a hard reference price for PayPal shares and signals renewed consolidation interest in payments.
insights.quote: a combined group which includes Stripe and a private equity firm uh called Advent for $53 billion.

insights.claim: PayPal fell from about $300 to about $40 a share over four years while Hedgeye held it short for most of that decline, then covered the short on June 29 and moved the name to a neutral 'bench' stance after its signal shifted from bearish to neutral just before the buyout news broke.
insights.speaker: Josh Steiner (financials analyst)
insights.so_what: Illustrates the value of scaling out of a long-running short as a signal shifts, even without knowing the specific catalyst (an M&A announcement) in advance.

insights.claim: Hedgeye's quantitative signal moved PayPal onto its long 'signal strength' list two to three days before the Stripe/Advent buyout news broke, illustrating that price/volume-based signals can front-run fundamental or M&A news without any inside information.
insights.speaker: Keith (host)
insights.so_what: Treat signal-list additions/removals as an early, legal proxy for positioning ahead of news, and act on the signal rather than waiting for analyst upgrades.
insights.quote: The signal two days ago said this was coming.

insights.claim: Compass (COMP) is a new highlighted long as a way to play falling mortgage rates tied to the broader CPI-rollover and disinflation call, with the stock's quantitative signal strength confirming the trade after a pullback tied to a recent interest-rate pop.
insights.speaker: Matt Cooper (real estate analyst)
insights.so_what: Own Compass as a levered, signal-confirmed proxy on the mortgage-rate-decline/disinflation thesis playing out elsewhere in the portfolio.

insights.claim: Lucid Group shares fell 50% and were halted after reports the company might go private or file for Chapter 11 (later denied by the company); Hedgeye has held it short since its 2021 SPAC deal, citing losses of over $300,000 per vehicle, perpetually negative gross margins, and roughly $1 billion per quarter in cash burn, with its Saudi PIF majority owner likely to keep funding it via further capital raises ahead of its lower-priced 'Cosmos' model rather than let it fail outright.
insights.speaker: auto analyst (Lucid coverage team)
insights.so_what: Broken unit economics can persist for years as long as a deep-pocketed majority owner keeps recapitalizing the company; don't expect a near-term bankruptcy despite negative cash flow, but the equity remains structurally impaired.
insights.quote: They lose over $300,000 per car uh and have like perpetually negative gross margins, burn one billion a quarter in cash burn.

insights.claim: US refining crack spreads are hitting record levels as diesel supply tightens — Ukrainian strikes are curtailing Russian refinery diesel exports while Russia shifts toward exporting crude instead — and US inventories continue to draw down, setting refiners up for very strong second- and third-quarter earnings, with much of the third-quarter margin already locked in given refining's production timing, and excess free cash flow expected to go toward buybacks.
insights.speaker: Fernando (energy analyst)
insights.so_what: Own US refiners into a multi-quarter run of record crack spreads and buyback-driven capital returns, largely independent of near-term oil price moves.
insights.quote: crack spreads are getting to record levels.

insights.claim: PJM (the Pennsylvania-Jersey-Maryland power market) failed for a third consecutive auction to procure enough committed peak generation capacity, and Hedgeye prefers independent power producers Vistra and Talen over regulated utilities, with a further preference for Texas-region power exposure over PJM given faster buildout timelines amid AI-driven demand growth.
insights.speaker: Fernando (energy analyst)
insights.so_what: Structural power-generation shortfalls from AI data-center demand favor merchant/independent power producers able to build quickly (Texas) over slower-moving regulated PJM utilities.
insights.quote: PJM, Pennsylvania, Jersey, Maryland uh region had their uh third consecutive auction where they couldn't get enough power for peak sides uh committed.

insights.claim: Google is rumored to release its Gemini 3.5 Pro model around July 17, after delaying the original June launch following the departure of AI researcher Noam Shazeer and other talent, and after running into agentic-use-case issues with the pre-trained version; Hedgeye's signal has already flipped Google back to a bullish trend, and a positive release is expected to support a Q2 revenue-growth acceleration narrative into earnings.
insights.speaker: software/internet analyst
insights.so_what: Watch for the Gemini 3.5 Pro release as a binary near-term catalyst for Google shares heading into Q2 earnings; a delay or muted reception would be a warning sign for the cloud/AI growth narrative.
insights.quote: we're getting a a potentially rumored release of the Gemini 3.5 Pro model on Friday.

insights.claim: A wave of competing AI model releases — including OpenAI's GPT-5.6 and an expected DeepSeek stable release around July 24 — means Google has a lot to prove to maintain its position across the AI frontier, as models across the industry get both cheaper to run and more capable at agentic tasks.
insights.speaker: software/internet analyst
insights.so_what: The AI model race is intensifying on both cost and capability fronts; Google's competitive position, not just its own execution, will determine whether it holds its AI leadership premium.

insights.claim: The February 'Mythos' model release initially crushed cybersecurity stocks on fears that AI would reduce future zero-day vulnerabilities, but instead forced enterprises to compress their security-spending timelines, which contributed to a cybersecurity-stock rally and played into IBM's guidance-driven stock plunge — its worst single day since 1987.
insights.speaker: software/internet analyst
insights.so_what: A feared AI-driven headwind for cybersecurity turned into a tailwind by accelerating enterprise security budgets; stay long the security-software complex into earnings season for confirmation.

insights.claim: AI agents are expanding the enterprise attack surface, which should keep driving durable demand for agent-identity and agent-observability security tooling, a theme Hedgeye has flagged alongside Datadog as an early beneficiary of AI/agent adoption across software.
insights.speaker: software/internet analyst
insights.so_what: Position in the 'agent layer' security and observability names as a second-derivative beneficiary of enterprise AI adoption, alongside the AI-infrastructure longs.
insights.quote: AI agents means more attack services in the long term.

insights.claim: Hedgeye's signal rotated CrowdStrike into the top third of its 93-name signal-strength list two days before it became one of the best-performing stocks in its coverage, at the same time the signal flagged the setup for IBM's guidance-driven plunge — its worst day since 1987 — illustrating the signal catching both a breakout and a blowup concurrently.
insights.speaker: Keith (host)
insights.so_what: Treat simultaneous signal moves across correlated names (e.g., security software up, a legacy IT vendor down) as a higher-confidence read on a sector rotation already underway.
insights.quote: the signal had us move crowd strike CRWD, which is the best of all those stocks yesterday, I think.

insights.claim: Enterprise adoption is increasingly migrating toward Chinese open-source AI models — 10 to 60 times cheaper than US equivalents — for workloads like coding agents, with DoorDash and Airbnb cited as companies already using them instead of OpenAI or Anthropic; Hedgeye has flagged this model-commoditization risk since January 2026, and it is now attracting mainstream media and political attention in both the US and China.
insights.speaker: Felix (semiconductor analyst)
insights.so_what: Model commoditization from cheap Chinese open-source alternatives is arriving faster than expected, pressuring the pricing power of premium US model providers and warranting a watch on which software/infrastructure names are most exposed.
insights.quote: these Chinese models are 10 to 60 times cheaper than the US equivalents.

insights.claim: New York's governor signed an executive order pausing (not banning) new data-center approvals, part of an emerging partisan split on AI infrastructure in which Republicans are generally more supportive of buildout and Democrats are increasingly divided due to reliance on big-tech campaign contributions; a likely compromise centers on regulating electricity and water use, with a more coordinated federal legislative effort not expected until after the midterms.
insights.speaker: Paul Glencher (policy analyst)
insights.so_what: Growing state-level opposition to data-center buildout (starting with a major state like New York) is an early-stage but rising regulatory risk to AI infrastructure capex and the broader AI trade; watch for spread to other states ahead of the midterms.
insights.quote: the governor signed an executive order. It's not a ban. It's just pausing them.
